answer:
Cynewulf's Andreas (cynewulf-andreas-07b573, ch. 1) and Iqbal's Bang-e-Dara stage the human-divine relationship in nearly opposite ways.

In Andreas, the divine is the active agent and human action is responsive obedience. The poem opens by naming the apostles 'twelve true thanes under the turning stars, the Lord's own champions,' whose 'lots were laid out as the Lord Himself, Heaven's High King, handed them their fates' — a feudal/heroic frame in which the Lord literally distributes destinies (ch. 1, p-868413). The narrative is propelled by direct divine command and intervention: God's voice tells the imprisoned Matthew to 'hold fast' (p-bdbda7), then commands Andrew to sail to Mermedonia (p-4d1e78); Andrew protests his frailty ('I am flesh, Lord, and frail is my knowledge', p-944879) and the Lord answers 'the path is not hard for a hero who serves me' (p-c4d450); Christ Himself disguises as the Helmsman of the ship (p-24dbdc). At the climax it is the saint's prayer that triggers a cosmic miracle — the flood obeys 'the holy one's command,' the mountain splits, the drowned youths are resurrected by his plea (p-839aab) — and the converted Mermedonians conclude 'Now it is seen that the true Maker, the King of all creatures, wields with His crafts, He who sent this messenger hither' (p-839aab). Human heroism is real but instrumental: thanes serve, suffer, and pray; God wields. The poem closes with the chorus 'One is the eternal God of all creation! His might and His majesty over middle-earth are famously blessed' (p-be1571).

Iqbal's Bang-e-Dara inverts the polarity. Humanity is the restless, world-making agent, and the divine is closer to the inner spring of that agency than to an external commander. In 'Sarguzasht-e-Adam' / The Saga of Man (Bang-E-Dara Part 1, ch. 37), Adam himself narrates a self-chosen exile: 'My spirit found no rest in Paradise's domain, once the fiery cup of consciousness I chose to drain'; he founds and topples idols, climbs Sinai, hangs on the Cross, sits in Hira, harnesses gravity and electricity, 'this earth into a rival of Paradise I wrought' — and only at the end finds that God 'in my own heart's house... did reside.' 'Insaan' / The Human Being (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 12) makes the metaphysics explicit: 'Whatever exists in the world is accustomed to submission; every power of the Human Being is aflame with striving... If it so chooses, it can change the very form of the garden; this being is wise, is perceptive, is potent.' The long Shikwa/Jawab-style 'Dil se jo baat nikalti hai asar rakhti hai' (Bang-E-Dara Part 3, ch. 4) dramatizes the relationship as dialogue rather than dictation: a defiant human voice reaches the Throne, and God, instead of issuing commands, reproaches Muslims for their inaction — 'You sit with hands upon your hands, and wait for futures'; promises that 'to one who's worthy, We grant kingly might'; and ends with the astonishing inversion 'Be true to Muhammad, and We are wholly thine; this world is nothing — Pen and Tablet of all Fate are thine.' Even the children's prayer 'Bachay Ki Dua' (Bang-E-Dara Part 1, ch. 16) is structurally outward-bound — 'may my life be like a candle's flare... may my life's work be to champion the poor' — petitioning God to enable striving rather than rescue.

The contrast: Andreas's divine economy is heteronomous and providential — God authors fate, dispatches saints, calms seas, raises the dead, and the human role is faithful obedience under a King. Iqbal's economy is dialogical and ethical — the human is the only being in creation that strives rather than submits, and God's relation to that human is to enable, test, and reproach, ultimately handing the 'Pen and Tablet of Fate' to the one who lives the faith. Where Cynewulf's hero says 'I am flesh, Lord' and is overruled by miracle, Iqbal's Adam says 'I chose to drain the cup of consciousness' and is answered with responsibility.
